Fulfillment Metrics for Retail. Let's rock and roll. So this is the Intelligent Metrics around Retail for Fulfillment. The first number that we're going to look at is Total Service Clients and we're going to include new clients, which means every single person who is in your gym who is paying for service. The next thing that we're going to track is Total Subscriptions. So you have X amount of clients and then you have X amount of people who are on subscription for physical products. Then we're going to look at percentage of clients on Subscrib&Save. And so what we're going to do here is just divide the total subscriptions by the total client. So if you have 10 clients on subscription and you have 20 clients, then 50% of your clients are on subscription. And the reason this is an important stat to track is that you can see over time that you can push that stat up and be like, guys, we need to get over 25%. We need to get over 50%. We need to get over 75%. Whatever it is, if you report that on a daily metric, for example, then that would be something that your entire team is going to work towards is getting everyone on subscription for a second revenue line. The next one is the flip side of this, which is the number of subscription cancellations. So how many do we have? How many people canceled their subscription within X period of time? And then you have attrition and or churn, which is going to be the number of cancellations divided by the total subscriptions. So if you had, let's say, 20 subscriptions and two of them canceled, then it'd be 2 divided by 20, which means you'd have a 10% churn. And so that's an important number because we're going to use that to calculate lifetime value. The reason this is important is one, you could like all these percentages are the things that you can push. You can push your team to increase the number of client percentage of clients on subscription. You can push them to decrease the amount of attrition or churn, which is going to come from making sure people are consuming them, answering questions or money, be able to take them all like this. So the next thing that would naturally to is lifetime value. And so there's two methods to do lifetime value and they're very similar to what I showed you before, but it's just worth going through it again for retail subscription. So you would be dividing the monthly average subscription divided by the three month average for churn. So why would I do the monthly average subscription instead of a three month average subscription? Mostly because you already have like, hopefully, you know, 70, 100 people on subscription. I mean, depending on the size of your business, but you should have a decent amount of people on subscription. So I'm going to give you a very good average number. If you have a smaller clientele base, let's say you only have 30 clients or something, then maybe you would add, you know, you do a three month average for your average subscription. So you'd average three averages together essentially, which is not actually the best way to do it. It would best way to do it would be to add all of them up together and then do one average. I don't want to get into it, but you can average one month and probably get a pretty good sample size to get your average subscription. And then the three month average churn is going to give you a good idea of because that will give you three months worth of data on the churn numbers. And so these might be some typical numbers that you might see, which is like a $220 average ticket for subscriptions and then 20% churn. So physical products and have IR churn, then service do, but that's still $1,100 lifetime value, which is almost the same as service just because the average ticket's higher, but the churn's higher. So it's kind of like you get both sides of it, right? Now the reason these things are important is because, and I'm going to show you the second one, but I'm going to tell you why they're important in a moment. So the second way to do this to calculate lifetime values, same thing monthly average subscription divided by cancellations, not times cancellations from the past three months. Okay, so you can see it in the equation below. I use the same numbers as I had before, which is two, five, 10, two, and four, which is 23 divided by five, 4.6 times the 220 number, which actually works out to almost the exact same lifetime value as before, which is kind of funny. I didn't do that on purpose, but it just, that's a different way of calculating the lifetime value. You're going to get two different numbers. I always take the lower one because I like to underestimate my upsides. Now the reason that having the lifetime value is so useful is because it does two things. One is it informs the extra value of subscribe and save, which is point two there. So it's like, if you know that you were going to take a $200 sale and it's going to become an $1100 sale, it's really worth taking the extra seconds to explain why it's a good benefit, why they should have it on a regular basis, why they should consume this, why this is going to be a part of their lifestyle, not like a trying thing, and really prescribe it to them. The second piece is it informs when to get paid in full. So if you know that your lifetime value on subscription is 4.6 months on average, then it doesn't actually make sense to push for the three month paid in fulls. And so it would only really make sense to go for the six or the 12s. So it's good to know your numbers because then you're maximizing how much revenue you're making from every sale and conversation because you have data, right? And then you make intelligent decisions and that's the whole point. So that's, I mean, that's like, for example, if you knew that your lifetime value of a customer for your, same thing for your service subscription is four months, then pushing to get a six month paid in full or a 12 month paid in full actually makes sense because you're actually getting more lifetime value out of the customer than you would be otherwise. So the key though is to make sure that if you do something like that, that you're not only taking the people who would have been twice that lifetime value, like in a service example, someone who was going to stay for two years and only buys a six month package, you really want to go for getting the people who, that's where you make the most money. It's making the difference of someone who wasn't going to stay that long and stays longer, right? Same thing with the subscription here. Somebody who may not have stayed for X months, but then stays twice as long because technically they're getting all the product upfront. So that is why lifetime value is important and that's a metric that you can track in and of itself so that it can form the selling decisions, why to push. And then also just overall, when you see how your churn decreases, you'll see the actual lifetime value go up, right? So if this all of a sudden went from 20% churn to 10% churn, then our lifetime value went up to 2200, right? Huge jumps and you can see how all these things work together into informing smarter business decisions.
